topic: The Milgram Experiment: Why 65% of People Will Kill on Command
hook: Most people will torture a stranger if asked
key_points: Stanley Milgram conducted his obedience study at Yale University in 1961
65% of subjects administered what they believed were lethal 450-volt shocks
Published in Journal of Abnormal and Social Psychology in 1963
Subjects continued despite hearing screams from the actor in the next room
The 'agentic state' theory explains why authority overrides personal ethics
Replications by Burger in 2009 showed nearly identical compliance rates
Modern parallels include corporate fraud and military atrocities

topic: Stanford Prison Experiment: How Normal Students Became Sadists in 6 Days
hook: A basement turned innocent students into monsters
key_points: Philip Zimbardo ran the experiment at Stanford in August 1971
Planned for 14 days but terminated after 6 due to escalating abuse
Guards developed sadistic behaviors within 36 hours
Prisoners showed signs of severe emotional breakdown by day 3
Zimbardo himself was caught up in his role as prison superintendent
Recent reanalysis suggests guards were coached, but situational power remains real
Influenced understanding of Abu Ghraib abuses decades later

topic: The Asch Conformity Experiments: Why You Agree With Lies You Know Are False
hook: Your brain caves to group pressure in seconds
key_points: Solomon Asch conducted his line-judgment studies at Swarthmore in 1951
75% of participants conformed to obviously wrong group answers at least once
Published in Groups, Leadership and Men by Carnegie Press
fMRI replications by Berns 2005 show conformity changes actual perception
Even one dissenter dropped conformity rates by 80%
Cultural variations exist but core finding holds globally
Explains social media echo chambers and political tribalism

topic: Cialdini's 6 Weapons of Influence: How Salespeople Hack Your Brain
hook: Six tricks that make you say yes automatically
key_points: Robert Cialdini published Influence: The Psychology of Persuasion in 1984
Reciprocity: Hare Krishnas raised millions giving away flowers first
Commitment and consistency: car dealers use lowball technique to lock buyers
Social proof: laugh tracks make jokes seem funnier even when not
Authority: lab coats increase compliance with absurd requests by 400%
Liking: Tupperware parties exploit friend-to-friend trust
Scarcity: 'Only 2 left' triggers ancient survival instincts

topic: The Bystander Effect: Why 38 People Watched Kitty Genovese Die
hook: More witnesses means less help arrives
key_points: Kitty Genovese was murdered in Queens NYC on March 13 1964
Original New York Times report claimed 38 witnesses did nothing
Darley and Latane studied diffusion of responsibility at Columbia 1968
Subjects took 6x longer to report a fire when others were present
Pluralistic ignorance: everyone assumes someone else will act
Recent journalism revealed only 2-3 actually saw the attack clearly
Modern smartphone era has changed but not eliminated the effect

topic: Confirmation Bias: Why Your Brain Refuses to See the Truth
hook: You only see evidence that proves you right
key_points: Peter Wason coined the term in 1960 with the 2-4-6 task
Subjects systematically sought confirming rather than disconfirming evidence
Published in Quarterly Journal of Experimental Psychology
Nickerson 1998 review documented effect across 30+ domains
Political beliefs amplify the bias by 2-3x in fMRI studies
Echo chamber algorithms exploit and amplify the bias daily
Karl Popper's falsification principle was the philosophical antidote

topic: Kahneman's System 1 and System 2: The Two Brains Inside You
hook: One brain is fast and lies to you
key_points: Daniel Kahneman published Thinking Fast and Slow in 2011
System 1: automatic intuitive emotional fast processing
System 2: deliberate analytical effortful slow processing
Won Nobel Prize in Economics in 2002 with Amos Tversky
Cognitive misers: brains default to System 1 to save energy
Anchoring effect: random numbers shift expert estimates by 30%
Most decisions including medical and judicial happen in System 1

topic: The Dunning-Kruger Effect: Why Idiots Think They're Geniuses
hook: Stupidity feels exactly like genius from inside
key_points: Justin Kruger and David Dunning published in JPSP 1999
Cornell University study on logic grammar and humor competence
Bottom 12% rated themselves in the 62nd percentile on average
Top performers underestimated themselves by about 15 percentile points
Metacognitive deficit: incompetence prevents recognizing incompetence
Recent statistical reanalyses suggest some artifact but core finding holds
Explains anti-vaxxers flat-earthers and overconfident politicians

topic: Dark Triad Personality: How to Spot Narcissists Psychopaths and Machiavellians
hook: Three personality types ruin most lives
key_points: Paulhus and Williams identified the cluster in JRP 2002
Narcissism: grandiosity entitlement need for admiration
Machiavellianism: strategic manipulation cynical worldview
Psychopathy: callousness impulsivity lack of empathy
Affects 1-3% of general population but 4% of CEOs (Babiak 2010)
Short Dark Triad SD3 questionnaire is the standard 27-item measure
Online dating apps overrepresent dark triad by 3x baseline

topic: Cognitive Dissonance: How Your Brain Lies to Itself to Feel Good
hook: Your brain rewrites memories to protect ego
key_points: Leon Festinger introduced theory at Stanford in 1957
Original cult study followed Marian Keech's UFO doomsday prediction
$1 vs $20 boring task experiment showed less pay equals more belief change
Brain's anterior cingulate cortex activates during dissonance (van Veen 2009)
Resolution strategies: change behavior change belief or rationalize
Smokers underestimate cancer risk to reduce dissonance
Sunk cost fallacy is dissonance reduction in real time

topic: The Halo Effect: Why Beautiful People Get Lighter Sentences
hook: Pretty faces fool judges juries and you
key_points: Edward Thorndike coined term in 1920 studying military officer ratings
Attractive defendants receive 22% shorter sentences (Stewart 1980)
Beautiful job candidates rated as more competent intelligent moral
Politicians rated attractive win 2.5x more elections (Todorov 2005)
Halo extends from one positive trait to unrelated positive judgments
Reverse devil effect works the same with negative traits
Tinder swipe behavior is halo effect in microseconds

topic: Operant Conditioning: How Slot Machines and TikTok Hijack Your Brain
hook: Random rewards create the strongest addictions
key_points: B.F. Skinner developed theory at Harvard in the 1930s and 1940s
Variable ratio reinforcement schedules produce highest response rates
Pigeons pecked 6000 times per hour for unpredictable food rewards
Slot machines deliberately use this exact schedule
Social media notifications mimic Skinner box mechanics precisely
Dopamine release peaks during anticipation not reward (Schultz 1997)
Tristan Harris and former Google designers admit deliberate exploitation

topic: The Bystander to Hero Pipeline: Why Some People Run Toward Danger
hook: Heroes share three rare psychological traits
key_points: Frank Farley's 'Big T' personality theory identifies thrill-seeking heroes
Carnegie Hero Fund Commission has documented 10000+ civilian heroes since 1904
Heroes show elevated empathy combined with low fear response
Samuel and Pearl Oliner studied Holocaust rescuers in The Altruistic Personality 1988
Rescuers shared an 'extensive' moral identity including out-groups
Brain imaging shows heroes' amygdala downregulates faster (Rand 2014)
Training and prior exposure increase odds of intervention 4x

topic: Anchoring Bias: How One Random Number Controls Your Wallet
hook: Any number you see warps your decisions
key_points: Tversky and Kahneman first documented anchoring in Science 1974
Spinning wheel set to 65 made experts estimate 45% African UN nations
Spinning wheel set to 10 made same experts estimate 25%
Restaurant menus place decoy expensive items to anchor mid-prices
Real estate listing prices anchor buyer offers within 8% (Northcraft 1987)
Even random Social Security numbers anchor bidding (Ariely 2003)
Negotiation: first offer wins 80% of final-price battles

topic: Gaslighting Tactics: 7 Ways Manipulators Make You Doubt Reality
hook: Seven phrases that erase your mind
key_points: Term originates from Patrick Hamilton's 1938 play Gas Light
Robin Stern coined modern usage in The Gaslight Effect 2007
Denial: 'That never happened' said with absolute conviction
Trivializing: 'You're being too sensitive' minimizes valid emotions
Counter-attack: shifting blame to keep victim defending themselves
Withholding: pretending not to understand to exhaust the target
Long-term victims show measurable cognitive decline (Sweet 2019)

topic: The Forer Effect: Why You Believe Horoscopes Are Personal
hook: Vague statements feel custom-made for you
key_points: Bertram Forer ran his classic experiment at college level in 1948
Students rated identical 'personal' descriptions as 4.26 of 5 accurate
Description was actually copied from a newsstand astrology book
Replicated hundreds of times with consistent results
Underlies cold reading by mediums fortune tellers and recruiters
Personality test industry is built on subjective validation
Combines confirmation bias selective memory and need for meaning

topic: Loss Aversion: Why Losing $100 Hurts More Than Winning $200 Feels Good
hook: Losses hurt twice as much as gains feel good
key_points: Kahneman and Tversky published Prospect Theory in Econometrica 1979
Losses weighted approximately 2.25x more than equivalent gains
Drives endowment effect: people demand more to sell than to buy
Investors hold losing stocks too long and sell winners too soon
Marketers use 'don't miss out' framing to trigger loss aversion
Tax framing: 'rebate' vs 'bonus' changes spending behavior 30%
Brain imaging shows amygdala activation peaks for losses not gains

topic: The Mere Exposure Effect: Why You Like Songs You First Hated
hook: Familiarity creates love automatically over time
key_points: Robert Zajonc demonstrated effect in JPSP 1968 monograph
Repeated exposure to nonsense syllables increased liking
Even subliminal exposures below conscious threshold work
Explains why pop radio plays the same songs hundreds of times
Political ads work mostly through mere exposure not persuasion
Reverses for already-disliked stimuli with prolonged exposure
Meta-analysis Bornstein 1989 confirmed across 200+ studies

topic: The Rosenhan Experiment: How 8 Sane People Fooled Psychiatric Hospitals
hook: Sane people locked in mental wards for weeks
key_points: David Rosenhan published 'On Being Sane in Insane Places' in Science 1973
8 healthy pseudopatients reported one false symptom each
All were admitted: 7 diagnosed with schizophrenia 1 with bipolar
Average stay was 19 days some up to 52 days
Once labeled normal behavior was reinterpreted as pathological
Real patients spotted them as fake while staff did not
Susannah Cahalan's 2019 book questioned Rosenhan's data integrity

topic: Pavlov's Dogs: How Classical Conditioning Programs Your Cravings
hook: Bells controlled dogs and ads control you
key_points: Ivan Pavlov won Nobel Prize 1904 for digestion research not psychology
Discovered conditioned reflex accidentally while measuring dog saliva
Bell paired with food eventually triggered salivation alone
Watson and Rayner's Little Albert 1920 conditioned fear in a baby
Marketing pairs brand logos with attractive imagery to transfer feelings
Drug addiction involves classical conditioning to environmental cues
Coca-Cola Christmas campaigns are textbook conditioning

topic: Just-World Hypothesis: Why People Blame Victims Automatically
hook: Your brain insists victims deserved their fate
key_points: Melvin Lerner introduced concept in JPSP 1965 shock experiments
Observers derogated suffering victims to maintain belief in justice
Stronger believers in just world blame rape victims and the poor more
Provides psychological comfort but enables systemic injustice
Lerner-Simmons Just World Scale measures the trait dimension
Correlates with right-wing authoritarianism (Furnham 2003)
Explains 'karma' beliefs and meritocracy myths in capitalism

topic: The Pygmalion Effect: How Your Expectations Shape Other People's Reality
hook: Believing in someone literally raises their IQ
key_points: Rosenthal and Jacobson published Pygmalion in the Classroom in 1968
Random students labeled as 'intellectual bloomers' gained 12 IQ points
Teachers unconsciously gave them more attention and better feedback
Effect replicated in military training courtrooms and workplaces
Meta-analysis Rosenthal 1991 confirmed across 345 studies
Reverse Golem effect: low expectations create low performers
Foundation of self-fulfilling prophecy in sociology and management

topic: Foot in the Door Technique: How Tiny Yes Becomes Massive Compliance
hook: One small yes opens door to giant yes
key_points: Freedman and Fraser published seminal study in JPSP 1966
Homeowners who agreed to small petition signed huge yard sign 76% of time
Control group without petition agreed only 17% of time
Self-perception theory: small commitment changes self-image
Cialdini incorporated as 'commitment and consistency' principle
Salespeople use email signups before pushing major purchases
Cult recruitment uses identical escalating commitment pattern

topic: Door in the Face Technique: How Big Refusals Lead to Bigger Compliance
hook: Asking for too much makes you say yes
key_points: Cialdini and colleagues published in JPSP 1975
Subjects asked to mentor delinquents 2 years refused at 100%
Same subjects then asked one zoo trip agreed at 50%
Control group asked only zoo trip agreed at 17%
Reciprocal concessions: refusal feels like rude rejection
Used in salary negotiation hostage scenarios and price haggling
Works only with same requester and reasonable second ask

topic: The Stanley Milgram Lost Letter Technique: Measuring Society's Hidden Prejudice
hook: Dropped letters reveal a city's racism
key_points: Stanley Milgram published the technique in Public Opinion Quarterly 1965
Letters addressed to controversial groups dropped on streets
Return rates measure latent attitudes without surveys
Letters to Nazi Party returned 25% less than medical research
Used by Bridges 2008 to detect anti-Muslim sentiment post-9/11
Modern variants include 'lost' phones and emails
Bypasses social desirability bias of self-report studies

topic: The Robbers Cave Experiment: How Easy It Is to Create Tribal Hatred
hook: Boys turned into warring tribes within days
key_points: Muzafer Sherif conducted study at Robbers Cave State Park Oklahoma 1954
22 11-year-old boys randomly split into Eagles and Rattlers
Within days groups developed flags chants and bitter hostility
Competition for prizes escalated to raids and physical fights
Superordinate goals (broken water supply) eventually reduced conflict
Realistic conflict theory explains modern intergroup violence
Recent investigations revealed Sherif manipulated some results

topic: Operation Mindfuck: The CIA's Real MK-Ultra Mind Control Program
hook: The CIA dosed citizens with LSD for years
key_points: Project MK-Ultra ran 1953-1973 under Sidney Gottlieb
1977 Senate Church Committee revealed 149 subprojects
CIA dosed brothel customers in San Francisco safe houses without consent
Ken Kesey LSD experiences came from a Veterans Hospital MK-Ultra trial
Frank Olson plunged from NYC hotel window after secret dosing 1953
Most files destroyed in 1973 by CIA director Richard Helms
Led to 1974 Privacy Act and 1981 EO 12333 restrictions

topic: The Marshmallow Test: What Self-Control at Age 4 Predicts About Your Life
hook: One marshmallow predicts your entire life
key_points: Walter Mischel ran original tests at Stanford Bing Nursery 1972
4-year-olds offered one marshmallow now or two in 15 minutes
1990 follow-up linked delay to higher SAT scores by 210 points
2011 brain imaging showed prefrontal cortex differences
2018 replication by Watts found effect smaller and confounded by SES
Strategies (cover candy distract) more important than raw willpower
Modern view: executive function trainable not fixed

topic: The IKEA Effect: Why You Overvalue Things You Built Yourself
hook: Building it makes you love it irrationally
key_points: Norton Mochon and Ariely published in Journal of Consumer Psychology 2012
Subjects valued IKEA boxes they assembled 63% higher than pre-built
Effect requires successful completion not just effort
Origami swans made by subjects valued like expert work
Build-a-Bear Workshop monetized the effect to $3 billion revenue
Contrasts with effort justification: completion needed
Explains overvaluation of homemade meals startup pitches and DIY

topic: Reactance Theory: Why Forbidding Something Makes Everyone Want It
hook: Banning a thing makes people crave it
key_points: Jack Brehm published A Theory of Psychological Reactance in 1966
Threats to freedom trigger motivational state to restore choice
Romeo and Juliet effect: parental disapproval increases attraction by 25%
Health warnings on cigarettes can paradoxically increase appeal
Reverse psychology exploits reactance deliberately
Explains anti-vaccine movements and teenage rebellion patterns
Mask mandates triggered measurable reactance backlash 2020

topic: The Spotlight Effect: Why You Think Everyone's Watching (They're Not)
hook: Nobody is watching you they're worried about themselves
key_points: Gilovich Medvec and Savitsky published in JPSP 2000
Subjects wearing embarrassing Barry Manilow shirts overestimated noticers by 100%
Average estimate: 50% noticed actual: 25%
Egocentric anchoring: hard to imagine others' perspective
Affects social anxiety speech anxiety and fashion choices
Reduces with cognitive behavioral training
Cure: deliberate awareness that others are also self-focused

topic: False Memory Implantation: How Easy It Is to Create Fake Memories
hook: Police questions can create memories from nothing
key_points: Elizabeth Loftus's lost in the mall study 1995 implanted false memories in 25%
Subjects later embellished fake mall-loss memories with rich detail
Innocence Project: 70% of overturned convictions involved eyewitness error
Reconsolidation: each memory recall is a rewrite opportunity
Therapist-induced 'recovered' memories of abuse caused 1990s satanic panic
Loftus testified for defense in McMartin Preschool and OJ Simpson trials
DRM paradigm shows false recall is universal not pathological

topic: Schadenfreude: The Neuroscience of Enjoying Others' Pain
hook: Your brain's pleasure center loves other people's failure
key_points: Term originates German 1880s: Schaden harm + Freude joy
Takahashi 2009 showed ventral striatum activates during others' misfortune
Effect amplified for envied or rival individuals
Social comparison theory: relative status improves with others' losses
Sports rivalries reality TV and celebrity gossip exploit it
Justice-based variant activates different neural circuits
Higher narcissism and lower self-esteem predict stronger schadenfreude

topic: The Hawthorne Effect: Why Watching People Changes Their Behavior
hook: Observation alone changes how people perform
key_points: Studies at Western Electric Hawthorne Works ran 1924-1932
Productivity rose with both more and less lighting
Change attributed to subjects knowing they were studied
Henry Landsberger named effect in 1958 reanalysis
Modern reanalyses suggest effect partly methodological artifact
Influences workplace surveillance security cameras and audits
Quantified self movement uses effect deliberately for self-improvement

topic: The Dark Side of Charisma: Why Magnetic People Often Destroy Lives
hook: Charisma can be a weapon not a gift
key_points: Sosik and colleagues distinguish socialized vs personalized charisma 1996
Personalized charismatic leaders pursue self-interest at follower cost
Jim Jones Charles Manson and Adolf Hitler scored extreme personalized
House and Howell measured Machiavellian charisma in Leadership Quarterly 1992
Followers experience identity fusion that overrides personal judgment
Brain imaging shows reduced critical thinking activation in fans
Cult research shows charismatic leaders peak then crash predictably

topic: Belief Perseverance: Why Facts Don't Change Minds Even With Evidence
hook: Showing proof makes people believe lies harder
key_points: Anderson Lepper Ross published in JPSP 1980
Subjects formed beliefs from fake data then refused to drop after debunking
Backfire effect Nyhan and Reifler 2010 showed corrections strengthened myths
Recent replications show backfire less common but persistence robust
Prior beliefs create motivated reasoning biases
Identity-protective cognition explains political fact resistance
Inoculation theory: pre-bunking works better than post-debunking

topic: The Power of Touch: How One Second of Contact Shapes Trust
hook: A brief touch dramatically increases compliance
key_points: Crusco and Wetzel waitress study published in PSPB 1984
Brief shoulder touch increased tips by 23%
Hornik 1992 showed shoppers touched bought more and rated store higher
Insula activation explains touch-trust connection (Morrison 2010)
Cultural variations: high-contact vs low-contact cultures vary 5x
Sales training programs deliberately incorporate touch principles
Pandemic touch deprivation linked to measurable mental health decline

topic: Diffusion of Responsibility in Groups: How Teams Make Worse Decisions
hook: More people in the room means worse choices
key_points: Janis coined 'groupthink' in Victims of Groupthink 1972
Bay of Pigs 1961 and Challenger 1986 disasters as case studies
Symptoms: illusion of invulnerability self-censorship and mindguards
Stoner risky shift studies showed groups take greater risks than individuals
Asch conformity combines with diffused responsibility
Devil's advocate role increases decision quality 30%
Modern Slack/Zoom meetings show same patterns digitally

topic: Implicit Bias: The Unconscious Prejudices Running Your Decisions
hook: Your brain holds prejudices you'd never admit
key_points: Greenwald McGhee Schwartz developed IAT in JPSP 1998
Implicit Association Test measures reaction-time bias to category pairings
Nosek Banaji 2002 found 75% of whites show pro-white implicit bias
Heart-attack patients receive 40% less aggressive treatment when Black (Green 2007)
Resume callback rates 50% higher for white-sounding names (Bertrand 2004)
Implicit-explicit dissociation: liberals show similar biases as conservatives
Recent meta-analyses question IAT predictive validity for behavior

topic: The Werther Effect: How Suicide News Coverage Triggers More Deaths
hook: One celebrity suicide creates a wave of deaths
key_points: David Phillips named effect in American Sociological Review 1974
Front-page suicide stories in NYT followed by 2.5% spike in suicides
Marilyn Monroe death August 1962 led to 200 additional US suicides
Robin Williams 2014 death increased suicides 9.85% for 4 months
Werther's Sorrows novel 1774 caused copycat wave Goethe regretted
WHO media guidelines aim to reduce contagion via reporting standards
Reverse Papageno effect: stories of crisis-survival reduce contagion

topic: Pluralistic Ignorance: When Everyone Privately Disagrees But Publicly Conforms
hook: Everyone hates it but pretends to love it
key_points: Floyd Allport coined concept in 1924 describing Prohibition support
Private opinions diverge from public expression due to false consensus
Prentice and Miller 1993 showed students overestimated peer drinking comfort
Underlies emperor's new clothes phenomenon and political bubbles
Authoritarian regimes maintain power through enforced pluralistic ignorance
Asch's lone dissenter shattered conformity by 80%
Modern social media amplifies via curated public personas

topic: The Backfire Effect: Why Correcting Misinformation Sometimes Makes It Worse
hook: Correcting lies can strengthen the lie
key_points: Nyhan and Reifler published in Political Behavior 2010
Conservatives shown WMD correction increased belief in WMDs
Identity-protective cognition triggers when threats hit core beliefs
Wood and Porter 2019 found backfire less common than first thought
Persistence of misinformation more reliable than active backfire
Prebunking and inoculation more effective than post-correction
Explains anti-vaccine resistance climate denial and election fraud beliefs

topic: Operant Schedules in Dating Apps: Why Tinder Is Designed Like a Slot Machine
hook: Tinder runs on slot machine math
key_points: Variable ratio reinforcement maximizes engagement
Match notifications follow Skinner's 1938 partial reinforcement schedule
Average user swipes 140 times per session (Statista 2023)
Co-founder Jonathan Badeen admitted slot machine inspiration in interviews
Dopamine release peaks at the swipe not the match
Ghost matches and partial visibility maintain engagement
Match group revenues passed $3.2B 2023 driven by behavioral hooks

topic: The Decoy Effect: How Restaurants Trick You Into Spending More
hook: A useless option makes you buy expensive
key_points: Huber Payne Puto coined 'asymmetric dominance' in JCR 1982
Adding inferior third option shifts preference 30-60%
Economist subscription study 1984: print-only and combo at $125 with web at $59
Almost all chose combo when web-only $125 added as decoy
Wine lists place expensive bottle to anchor mid-range as 'reasonable'
Movie popcorn small/medium/large pricing exploits decoy effect
Apple iPhone tier pricing follows identical pattern

topic: The Endowment Effect: Why You Value Your Stuff More Than It's Worth
hook: Owning makes objects feel twice as valuable
key_points: Thaler proposed in Journal of Economic Behavior 1980
Knetsch coffee mug experiment 1989: owners demanded $7 sellers paid $3
Loss aversion explanation: giving up feels like loss
Prospect theory grounding: reference point shifts upon ownership
Free trials exploit endowment to create reluctance to return
Real estate listing prices reflect owner endowment biases
Disappears under expert traders (List 2003) suggesting learnable

topic: Subliminal Messaging: What Actually Works and What's Marketing Myth
hook: Most subliminal claims are total bullshit
key_points: James Vicary's 1957 popcorn theater study was admitted hoax in 1962
Karremans 2006 showed subliminal Lipton Ice priming worked only on thirsty subjects
Greenwald 1996 meta-analysis: subliminal effects exist but are tiny and brief
Self-help subliminal tapes show no effect over placebo
Brain imaging confirms unconscious processing of stimuli (Dehaene 2014)
FCC banned subliminal advertising 1974 despite weak evidence
Modern algorithmic curation may be more powerful than classical subliminal

topic: Triangulation: The Manipulator's Tool to Control Multiple Victims
hook: Manipulators turn victims against each other
key_points: Murray Bowen developed family systems theory 1960s
Triangulation introduces third party to manage two-person tension
Narcissists use triangulation to maintain narcissistic supply
Common in custody battles workplace politics and abusive relationships
Smear campaigns recruit allies against the targeted person
Karpman drama triangle 1968 maps victim/persecutor/rescuer roles
Sets up gaslighting through manufactured social validation

topic: The Curse of Knowledge: Why Experts Are Bad at Teaching
hook: Knowing too much makes you a worse explainer
key_points: Camerer Loewenstein Weber published in Journal of Political Economy 1989
Newton Elizabeth's 1990 tappers and listeners study revealed massive overestimation
Tappers thought 50% of listeners would identify songs actual rate 2.5%
Once you know something you cannot imagine not knowing it
Underlies bad documentation API design and academic writing
Made to Stick by Heath brothers 2007 popularized the concept
Counters: rubber duck explanation user testing and Feynman technique

topic: Mirror Neurons: How Your Brain Catches Other People's Emotions
hook: Your brain copies whatever it watches
key_points: Discovered by Rizzolatti team at Parma University 1990s in macaques
Same neurons fire when monkey acts and when monkey observes action
Human studies use fMRI as direct recording impossible
Hypothesized mechanism for empathy language and autism
Hickok 2014 critiques overstating role of mirror neurons in empathy
Yawn contagion is the simplest demonstration
Performance arts use mirror neurons to transmit emotion

topic: The Bandwagon Effect: How Social Proof Creates Election Landslides
hook: Polls showing wins create more wins
key_points: Henri Leibenstein wrote about bandwagon demand QJE 1950
Voters shift toward predicted winners by 2-5 percentage points
Spiral of silence: minority opinions suppress themselves over time
Stock market bubbles run on bandwagon dynamics
Ash conformity Asch 1951 underlying mechanism
Tipping points cascade rapidly past 25% adoption (Centola 2018)
Social media amplifies bandwagon via visible engagement metrics

topic: The Backbone of Cult Recruitment: Three Stages Every Cult Uses
hook: Cults follow a predictable three-step formula
key_points: Steven Hassan's BITE model: Behavior Information Thought Emotion control
Stage 1 Love Bombing: overwhelming attention and acceptance
Stage 2 Isolation: cutting off outside relationships gradually
Stage 3 Reframing: introducing doctrine and us-vs-them worldview
Robert Cialdini's commitment escalation parallels cult induction
Margaret Singer's Cults in Our Midst 1995 documented patterns
Modern parallels in MLM extreme political movements and online communities

topic: The Bobo Doll Experiment: How Children Learn Violence by Watching
hook: Kids copy adult violence within minutes
key_points: Albert Bandura ran study at Stanford 1961 published in JASP 1965
Children who watched adults attack a Bobo doll mimicked aggressive acts
Boys imitated physical aggression girls verbal aggression
Founded social learning theory and observational learning
Spawned decades of TV violence research
American Psychological Association 2015 confirmed media-aggression link
Modern relevance: video games and influencer culture replicate effect

topic: Negative Reinforcement vs Punishment: What Most People Get Wrong
hook: Most people confuse two opposite concepts
key_points: B.F. Skinner distinguished four operant categories 1953
Negative reinforcement removes aversive stimulus to increase behavior
Punishment adds aversive stimulus to decrease behavior
Seatbelt buzzer is negative reinforcement (silenced when buckled)
Speeding ticket is positive punishment (added consequence)
Most parents and teachers use punishment thinking it's reinforcement
Long-term punishment creates avoidance not behavior change

topic: The Sunk Cost Fallacy: Why Smart People Keep Throwing Good Money After Bad
hook: Past losses control your future choices
key_points: Arkes and Blumer published in Organizational Behavior 1985
Subjects more likely to attend bad concert if they paid for tickets
Concorde fallacy: continued investment despite known losses
Vietnam War escalation analyzed as collective sunk cost (McCain 1986)
Personal relationships marriages and careers continue past expiration
Brain imaging shows ventromedial PFC activation in sunk cost decisions
Expert traders and economists also fall for it

topic: Cold Reading: How Mediums and Pickup Artists Manipulate Anyone
hook: Mediums use 4 tricks to fool anyone
key_points: Ray Hyman's Cold Reading 1977 documented techniques
Shotgunning: rapid vague statements seeing what sticks
Barnum statements: universal personality descriptions feel personal
Forer effect makes generic descriptions feel custom
Reading micro-expressions for confirmation cues
Pickup artist communities packaged techniques as 'cold reads'
Skeptic James Randi exposed John Edward Sylvia Browne and others

topic: Learned Helplessness: How Repeated Failure Programs Permanent Defeat
hook: Repeated failure rewires your brain to give up
key_points: Seligman and Maier studied dogs in inescapable shock 1967
After uncontrollable shocks dogs failed to escape escapable ones
Translated to human depression model in Seligman 1972
Maier 2016 reversal: helplessness is the default and control is learned
Found in abused children domestic violence victims and chronic poverty
Prefrontal cortex inhibits dorsal raphe to enable control learning
Foundation of positive psychology and learned optimism theory

topic: The Halo of Wealth: Why Rich People Are Trusted More Than They Should Be
hook: Money makes you believe expensive lies
key_points: Piff Stancato Cote 2012 showed wealthy participants cheated more
Yet observers rated them more competent and trustworthy
Stanford expensive wine study 2008: same wine rated better at high price
Plassmann fMRI 2008 confirmed brain pleasure centers actually activate more
Veblen 1899 theory of leisure class predicted status signaling
Bernie Madoff's $65B fraud relied on wealthy halo effect
Influencer marketing exploits wealth halos systematically

topic: Scarcity Mindset: How Poverty Permanently Damages Decision Making
hook: Poverty steals your IQ in real time
key_points: Mullainathan and Shafir published Scarcity 2013
Indian sugarcane farmers tested 13 IQ points lower pre-harvest than post
Cognitive bandwidth consumed by financial worry
New Jersey shoppers given $300 medical bill scenario showed similar drop
Tunneling: focus on immediate scarcity blocks long-term planning
Explains why poverty interventions need cash transfers not just education
GiveDirectly evidence supports unconditional transfers

topic: The Foot Soldier Effect: How Ordinary People Become Genocide Killers
hook: Average people commit atrocities under pressure
key_points: Christopher Browning's Ordinary Men 1992 studied Reserve Police Battalion 101
500 middle-aged German policemen killed 38000 Jews in Poland 1942
Most had no Nazi ideology only conformity and obedience
Hannah Arendt's banality of evil from Eichmann trial 1963
Browning identified peer pressure career incentives and brutalization
Goldhagen disputed with willing executioners thesis
Modern parallels in Rwanda Cambodia and Bosnia documented

topic: Microexpressions: The Half-Second Truths Your Face Always Reveals
hook: Your face leaks truth in 1/25 second
key_points: Paul Ekman developed Facial Action Coding System 1978
Discovered universal microexpressions across cultures including isolated tribes
7 universal emotions: anger fear disgust surprise sadness contempt happiness
Microexpressions last 1/15 to 1/25 of a second
Trained observers detect deception at 70% vs untrained 54% (Ekman 1991)
TSA SPOT program based on microexpressions criticized as ineffective
Lie to Me TV show popularized but exaggerated science

topic: The False Consensus Effect: Why You Think Everyone Agrees With You
hook: Your brain assumes others share your views
key_points: Lee Ross coined term in JESP 1977
Subjects who would wear sandwich board guessed 62% would also vs 33% non-wearers
Anchoring own opinion as reference point
Social media echo chambers amplify the effect
Predicts political polling errors and election misjudgments
Pluralistic ignorance is opposite (assuming others differ)
Reduces with deliberate exposure to diverse opinions

topic: Inattentional Blindness: How a Gorilla Walks Past Without You Seeing
hook: A gorilla walks past you and you miss it
key_points: Simons and Chabris published in Perception 1999
Subjects counting basketball passes missed person in gorilla suit
50% of viewers failed to notice unexpected stimulus
Mack and Rock 1998 named 'inattentional blindness'
Radiology study Drew 2013 showed 83% of doctors missed gorilla on CT
Magicians exploit inattention as core principle
Driver distraction studies confirm same blindness with phones

topic: The Identifiable Victim Effect: Why One Story Beats a Million Statistics
hook: One face beats one million dead statistics
key_points: Schelling 1968 noted preference for identified vs statistical victims
Small Loewenstein Slovic 2007: subjects donated more to one named child than statistics
Stalin attributed quote: one death tragedy million deaths statistic
Charity sector built around identifiable victim storytelling
Pseudo-inefficacy: adding statistics actually reduces donations
Brain imaging shows amygdala response to faces not numbers
Explains migrant crisis coverage and disease awareness campaigns

topic: Operant Conditioning of Children: The Hidden Cost of Reward Systems
hook: Rewarding good behavior often destroys it
key_points: Lepper Greene Nisbett 1973 showed overjustification effect
Children rewarded for drawing reduced spontaneous drawing 50%
Extrinsic rewards undermine intrinsic motivation
Deci and Ryan's Self-Determination Theory builds on findings
Sticker charts star systems and trophies for participation create dependence
Cognitive evaluation theory explains autonomy as key
Gamification of work shows similar long-term costs

topic: The Paradox of Choice: Why More Options Make You Miserable
hook: More choices make you feel worse
key_points: Barry Schwartz published The Paradox of Choice in 2004
Iyengar Lepper jam study 2000: 24 jams attracted shoppers but 6 jams sold more
Decision fatigue depletes glucose and willpower
Maximizers (seek best) less happy than satisficers (seek good enough)
Counterfactual regret amplifies with options
Recent meta-analysis Scheibehenne 2010 questioned strength of effect
Netflix paralysis and Tinder fatigue exemplify modern overload

topic: The Power of Names: How Your Name Shapes Your Career and Life
hook: Your name determines what job you get
key_points: Nelson and Simmons published 'Moniker Maladies' Psychological Science 2007
Dennis and Denise overrepresented as dentists by 9-11%
Bertrand Mullainathan 2004: white names received 50% more callbacks than Black names
Name initials predict career fields above chance
Implicit egotism: people prefer things resembling themselves
Recent replications question implicit egotism effect size
Easy-to-pronounce names boost stock IPO performance (Alter 2006)

topic: Group Polarization: How Discussion Makes Moderates Into Extremists
hook: Group talk turns moderates into extremists
key_points: Stoner 1961 'risky shift' began study of group polarization
Moscovici Zavalloni 1969 generalized to attitude polarization
Groups of like-minded individuals shift toward more extreme positions
Cass Sunstein 2009 applied to internet echo chambers
Mechanisms: social comparison and persuasive arguments
Online radicalization on YouTube TikTok and Telegram follows pattern
Janis groupthink overlaps with polarization effects

topic: Self-Serving Bias: Why Success Is Yours But Failure Is Someone Else's Fault
hook: You take credit and blame others always
key_points: Fritz Heider attribution theory 1958 laid groundwork
Miller Ross 1975 meta-analysis confirmed self-serving attributions
Athletes attribute wins to skill losses to bad calls or luck
CEOs in annual reports take credit for gains blame economy for losses
Self-esteem maintenance hypothesis is leading explanation
Reduced in depressed individuals (depressive realism)
Cultural variations: less pronounced in collectivist cultures

topic: The Power of Eye Contact: How Three Seconds Changes Brain Chemistry
hook: Three seconds of eye contact rewires you
key_points: Kellerman Lewis Laird 1989 showed mutual eye contact increased reported love
Two-minute eye gazing in lab created romantic feelings in strangers
Aron 1997 36-questions study with eye contact replicated effects
Oxytocin release peaks during eye contact (Nagasawa 2015 dog-human study)
Average comfortable eye contact lasts 3.2 seconds (Binetti 2016)
Autistic individuals show reduced amygdala activation to eye contact
Public speaking trainers prescribe 5-second eye contact bursts

topic: The Authority Bias: Why You Trust White Coats and Uniforms Blindly
hook: Lab coats and uniforms hijack your judgment
key_points: Bickman 1974 showed pedestrians obeyed guard uniform 92% vs civilian 19%
Hofling 1966 nurse study: 21 of 22 nurses overdosed patient on doctor phone order
Milgram experiment ran on white-coat scientist authority
fMRI Berns 2005 showed expert advice deactivated independent decision making
Halo extends from costume to character (Cialdini 1984)
Scams routinely exploit fake badges fake doctors fake officials
Combat with: ask credentials separate role from claims

topic: The Robbers Cave Followup: How Cooperation Erased Tribal Hatred
hook: Working together erased deep group hatred
key_points: Sherif's third phase 1954 introduced superordinate goals
Joint effort to fix water tank reduced hostility 40%
Truck-stuck-in-mud collaboration accelerated reconciliation
Contact alone insufficient: required interdependent goals
Allport 1954 contact hypothesis refined by Pettigrew 1998
Modern integration programs use shared-goal sports and projects
Workplace diversity programs follow same principles

topic: The Two-Year-Old Test: Why Toddler Behavior Reveals Adult Personality
hook: Your 3-year-old self predicts your 30-year-old self
key_points: Caspi Silva 1995 Dunedin study tracked 1037 children since 1972
Behavioral profiles at age 3 predicted personality at age 26
Undercontrolled toddlers became impulsive risk-taking adults
Inhibited toddlers became cautious depressed adults
Roberts 2006 meta-analysis: personality stability rises with age
Big Five trait scores show 0.7 correlation across decades
Genes account for ~40% with environment shaping the rest

topic: Stockholm Syndrome: When Hostages Defend Their Captors
hook: Victims fall in love with their abusers
key_points: Term coined after 1973 Norrmalmstorg bank robbery in Stockholm
Hostages defended robbers and refused to testify after 6-day siege
Patty Hearst 1974 Symbionese Liberation Army case popularized concept
Frank Ochberg formalized criteria for FBI in 1978
Not in DSM but recognized in trauma bonding literature
Domestic abuse cycles show similar attachment patterns
Critics question diagnostic validity and overuse of label

topic: The Mozart Effect: The Most Famous Lie in Pop Psychology
hook: Mozart does not make babies smarter
key_points: Rauscher Shaw 1993 study showed brief 8-9 IQ point boost in college students
Effect lasted 10-15 minutes only and only on spatial tasks
Don Campbell 1997 book extrapolated wildly to babies
Georgia governor 1998 distributed Mozart CDs to newborns
Chabris 1999 meta-analysis found effect minimal and not Mozart-specific
Any preferred music produced same arousal-based boost
FTC and journals officially debunked baby Mozart claims

topic: Operant Behavior in Modern Workplaces: How Bosses Train Employees Like Pigeons
hook: Your boss trains you like a Skinner box
key_points: B.F. Skinner's behaviorism applied to industrial-organizational psychology
Variable reinforcement schedules drive employee engagement metrics
Bonus structures use ratio schedules: most engaging but most stressful
Email notifications mimic Skinner box partial reinforcement
Aubrey Daniels' Performance Management 1989 systematized OBM
Amazon warehouse rate metrics show modern operant control
OBM critiques warn of dehumanization and burnout

topic: Hindsight Bias: Why Everyone Knew It Would Happen After It Happened
hook: Everyone predicts the past after it happens
key_points: Baruch Fischhoff coined 'creeping determinism' in 1975
Subjects told outcomes recalled their predictions as more accurate
Effect documented in medical diagnoses sports outcomes and politics
Roese Vohs 2012 meta-analysis: hindsight bias is robust universal
Underlies blame for missed warnings (9/11 Pearl Harbor)
Investment advisors and pundits exploit retrospective storytelling
Pre-mortem technique counters by forcing prospective thinking

topic: The Truth Effect: Why Repeated Lies Become Truth in Your Brain
hook: Repeating a lie makes brain accept truth
key_points: Hasher Goldstein Toppino published in JVLVB 1977
Repeated statements rated more truthful regardless of accuracy
Fluency hypothesis: easier processing feels like truth
Effect works on plausible and implausible statements alike (Fazio 2015)
Underpins propaganda advertising and disinformation campaigns
Goebbels' big lie technique exploited the effect
Brain imaging shows reduced prefrontal monitoring on repeats

topic: The Attractive Defendant Effect: How Beautiful Criminals Get Lighter Sentences
hook: Pretty defendants get pretty short sentences
key_points: Stewart 1980 observed 67 male defendants in Pennsylvania courts
Attractive defendants got 22% shorter sentences than unattractive
Replicated by Mazzella Feingold 1994 meta-analysis 80 studies
Mock juries treat attractive defendants leniently except in fraud cases
Effect amplified for traditionally feminine attractiveness
Babchishin 2014 showed effect size halved when crime is severe
Disrupts justice system fairness and equal protection

topic: The Eichmann Lessons: Hannah Arendt and the Banality of Evil
hook: Evil's most common form is utterly banal
key_points: Hannah Arendt covered Adolf Eichmann's 1961 Jerusalem trial for The New Yorker
Eichmann in Jerusalem published 1963 introduced 'banality of evil'
Eichmann was no monster but bureaucrat following orders
Arendt argued thoughtlessness enabled mass murder
Bettina Stangneth 2014 disputed showing Eichmann's anti-Semitic conviction
Concept influences modern study of corporate and political evil
Connects to Milgram obedience and Browning's ordinary men

topic: Cognitive Reframing: How CBT Hacks Your Own Brain Out of Depression
hook: Rewriting thoughts physically rewires your brain
key_points: Aaron Beck developed Cognitive Therapy at Penn 1960s
Cognitive triad of depression: negative views of self world future
Cognitive distortions: catastrophizing personalizing all-or-nothing
Hofmann 2012 meta-analysis: CBT effective in 269 studies of anxiety/depression
Brain imaging shows CBT changes prefrontal-amygdala circuits
Beck Depression Inventory remains gold standard measurement
Modern apps deliver CBT digitally with measurable outcomes

topic: The Tit for Tat Strategy: Game Theory's Solution to Human Cooperation
hook: One simple rule beats every strategy ever
key_points: Robert Axelrod hosted tournaments published Evolution of Cooperation 1984
Anatol Rapoport's tit-for-tat won both 1980 tournaments
Strategy: cooperate first then copy opponent's previous move
Properties: nice retaliating forgiving non-envious
Generous tit-for-tat with 10% forgiveness wins noisy environments
Models predator-prey political alliances and trade negotiations
Social media outrage cycles violate forgiveness component

topic: The Power of Storytelling: Why Narratives Override Statistics in Your Brain
hook: One story beats one thousand statistics
key_points: Paul Zak's neuroeconomics research 2013 showed oxytocin release during stories
Character-driven stories changed donation behavior in lab
Brain imaging Hasson 2008 showed listener-speaker neural coupling
Identifiable victim effect rooted in narrative processing
Aristotle's Poetics 335 BCE identified elements still used
Modern marketing and political messaging built on narrative
Counter: deliberately seek base rates and statistics

topic: The Need for Closure: Why Some People Cant Tolerate Uncertainty
hook: Some brains literally cannot tolerate doubt
key_points: Arie Kruglanski developed Need for Closure Scale 1993
Scale measures preference for predictability decisiveness order
High NFC associated with authoritarian political beliefs (Jost 2003)
Time pressure and noise increase situational closure need
High closure leads to seizing on first explanation and freezing
Predicts conspiracy belief and resistance to ambiguous evidence
Reduces with deliberate practice in negative capability (Keats 1817)

topic: Operant Extinction: Why Ignoring Bad Behavior Sometimes Makes It Worse
hook: Ignoring tantrums temporarily makes them worse
key_points: B.F. Skinner described extinction burst in operant principles 1938
Removed reinforcement triggers temporary surge in target behavior
Toddler tantrum gets louder before quieter when ignored
Spontaneous recovery: extinguished behavior returns briefly later
Vending machine test: violent shaking after coin loss is extinction burst
Used in ABA therapy for autism with mixed ethical reviews
Variable reinforcement creates extinction-resistant behaviors

topic: The Conformity of Crowds: How Riots Spread Like Viruses
hook: One person starting a riot triggers many
key_points: Mark Granovetter threshold model published in AJS 1978
Each person has individual threshold for joining riot
Cascading effect once early adopters with low thresholds act
Stanley Milgram crowd-staring study 1969 showed 80% lookup with 15 starters
Gustave Le Bon 1895 The Crowd identified crowd anonymity effects
Online mobs follow same threshold dynamics
January 6 Capitol breach and 2011 London riots fit threshold models

topic: Implicit Egotism: Why You Marry People With Your Initials
hook: You secretly love your own name letters
key_points: Pelham Mirenberg Jones published in JPSP 2002
Frequency of own letters in spouse's name above chance
Career choice: people named Dennis become dentists 23% above base
Cities resembling first name overrepresented as residence
Implicit self-esteem mechanism unconscious name preference
Anseel and Duyck 2008 replicated in Belgium with same patterns
Recent reanalyses by Simonsohn 2011 challenged effect size

topic: Acquired Sociopathy: How Brain Damage Creates Predators
hook: A head injury can create a real psychopath
key_points: Phineas Gage 1848 railroad spike injury changed personality drastically
Antonio Damasio's Descartes' Error 1994 reanalyzed Gage neuroanatomy
Ventromedial prefrontal cortex damage produces 'acquired sociopathy'
Patients normal IQ but lack emotional decision making
Iowa Gambling Task reveals VMPFC dysfunction quickly
Tumors in this region have caused murder and pedophilia in adults
Burns Swerdlow 2003 Texas case removed tumor reversed pedophilia

topic: The Stranger Effect: Why People Tell Strangers Their Deepest Secrets
hook: People tell strangers their darkest secrets
key_points: Zick Rubin 1975 documented strangers-on-a-train phenomenon
Anonymous one-time encounters reduce social risk of disclosure
Distance between disclosure and reputation maintains safety
Aron 36-questions 1997 showed strangers can simulate intimacy fast
Anonymous online forums (Reddit confessions) exploit same dynamic
Therapy effective partly because therapist is structured stranger
Compared to friends/family who carry social-stake judgment

topic: The Power of Smell on Memory: Why One Whiff Triggers a Decade of Memories
hook: One smell unlocks decades of buried memory
key_points: Marcel Proust's madeleine scene 1913 popularized concept
Olfactory bulb has direct connection to amygdala and hippocampus
Other senses route through thalamus first
Herz Schooler 2002 confirmed Proustian memories more emotional
Childhood smell memories peak around ages 6-10 (reminiscence bump)
PTSD often triggered by smell more than other senses
Aromatherapy studies show modest effects via memory association

topic: Operant Conditioning of Public Opinion: How Repetition Manufactures Belief
hook: Repetition turns opinions into facts in months
key_points: Edward Bernays Propaganda 1928 systematized PR techniques
Goebbels formalized 'big lie' repetition principle 1933
Hasher truth effect 1977 confirms scientific basis
Cable news repetition cycles maintain agenda for weeks
Twitter algorithm rewards repetition through engagement loops
Russian disinformation studies show 5-7 exposure threshold for belief
Counter: source diversity and active fact-checking habits
